title: Sage and onion stuffing
subtitle: The proper bread-based version, baked in a tray
excerpt: Soft breadcrumbs, sweet cooked onion, and plenty of dried sage bound with egg and butter, baked until golden and slightly crisp on top. The one that belongs on the Sunday-lunch table alongside roast pork or chicken.
sourceNotes: Classic British stuffing documented in Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — 'Sage-and-Onion Stuffing for Geese, Ducks, and Pork'. The recipe is largely unchanged in the modern British kitchen.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before baking: shape into balls or press into the tray, cover with cling film, and freeze for up to 3 months. Bake from frozen at 190°C for 35 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch and freeze half before baking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The mixture can be prepared up to 24 hours ahead and kept covered in the fridge. Bring to room temperature before baking.

content.text: Sage and onion stuffing made from a packet tastes primarily of dried herbs and salt. The version made from stale breadcrumbs, properly sweated onion, and enough fresh sage to be unmistakable is a different proposition: soft inside, slightly crunchy on the surface, and worth fighting over. It takes twenty minutes of active work.

Baking it in a tray rather than inside the bird produces a more even texture and allows the top to crisp. If you're cooking a chicken or pork joint and want to stuff the cavity, this mixture works there too, though add 10 minutes to the cooking time and check the centre reaches 75°C.

items.prepNote: or fresh breadcrumbs from a day-old loaf
fresh leaves, finely chopped (or 2 tsp dried)

content.text: Preheat the oven to 190°C fan. Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of unsalted butter in a frying pan over medium-low heat. Add {{onion}} finely chopped onions and cook, stirring occasionally, for 12 to 15 minutes until soft and translucent. They should not colour. Remove from the heat and leave to cool slightly.

Tip the softened onions into a large bowl with the pan juices. Add {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs, {{sage}} of finely chopped fresh sage, {{eggs}} beaten egg, and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Season generously with salt and pepper. Mix well; the mixture should hold together when pressed but not be wet or sloppy. Add a splash more stock if it seems dry.

Press the mixture into a lightly buttered rectangular baking tin in an even layer roughly 3 cm deep. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until golden brown on top and firm to the touch. Cut into squares to serve.

items.cause: Too little stock, or the breadcrumbs absorbed too much
items.fix: Stir in more stock before baking, a tablespoon at a time, until the mixture just holds together when squeezed.

items.symptom: Stuffing is soggy in the middle
items.cause: Too much liquid, or the tin was too deep
items.fix: Spread the mixture more thinly — a 2 to 3 cm layer is ideal. Extend the baking time in 5-minute increments.

items.cause: Dried sage was old and its oils had dissipated
items.fix: Next time use fresh sage, or replace dried sage more frequently. Dried sage loses pungency quickly after opening.

items.cause: Oven temperature was too low or the top needs more time
items.fix: Return to the oven for 10 more minutes, or finish under the grill for 2 to 3 minutes.

content.text: Sage and onion stuffing is the default British stuffing, carried as much by tradition as by flavour. Beeton's recipe in 1861 includes it for geese, ducks, and pork — the same meats it accompanies today. The packet version emerged in the post-war period when convenience cooking reshaped the British kitchen; the homemade version never entirely disappeared, and in households where the Sunday roast is taken seriously, it remains standard. The baked-in-a-tray approach replaced the inside-the-bird method partly for food-safety reasons (stuffed cavities are harder to heat through evenly) and partly because the crisp top is generally considered the best part.
